title: Mandatory registration begins for NDIS providers
full_text:
Today marks the start of the first stage of mandatory registration for providers in the National Disability Insurance Scheme.
Providers of digital platforms and supported independent living homes will be required to register with the NDIS watchdog.
Barbara Miller: Today marks the start of the first stage of mandatory registration for providers in the National Disability Insurance Scheme. The initial changes impact digital platforms and supported independent living homes. Here's National Work reporter Bronwyn Herbert.
Bronwyn Herbert: Cairns-based disability support worker Andrew Horton worries helping a client with everyday tasks could soon come with a substantial price tag.
Andrew Horton: It does feel like we're being asked to prepare for a cyclone but it could be a category one or it could be a category five.
Bronwyn Herbert: There is broad backing for the first wave of changes starting today that will force providers of digital platforms and supported independent living to register with the NDIS watchdog. But many small providers, independent support workers like Andrew Horton and families are increasingly anxious about the next stage of registration to begin next year, covering providers of high risk support. Andrew Horton estimates the cost for him will be around $10,000. Disability advocates argue that unless the government adopts a risk proportionate tiered model recommended by its own task force in 2024, the reforms will trigger an exodus of small providers who are unable to cover the costs of the registration. Carly Nisner from the Support Workers Association says there's fear and confusion across the sector.
Carly Nisner: And there's a lot of providers out there that are panicking, thinking they have to meet a 1st of July deadline, they have to be operating under those new practice standards by then, but they don't actually know what they will be because they're hard to find and they're still drafted, they're not finalised.
Bronwyn Herbert: But the changes are necessary, says Michael Perusco, Chief Executive of the peak body for NDIS providers, National Disability Services.
Michael Perusco: So we see registration as key to understanding who is it that is providing services in a $50 billion scheme. But the transition to a more regulated market needs to be handled carefully.
Bronwyn Herbert: The Minister for the NDIS, Jenny McAllister, says she will listen to concerns as the transition happens.
Jenny McAllister: We'll consult with people with disability and with the provider community as we work towards that deadline next year in 2027.
Bronwyn Herbert: In Kalgoorlie, Jo Russell coordinates a team supporting her daughter Megan. She's worried about what the changes will mean for self-managed care arrangements relied on by families like hers.
Jo Russell: She requires assistance with all aspects of daily living, including showering, grooming, cooking, shopping, eating. She needs support to help her to navigate the community because she has no social boundaries. So to keep her safe, she has to have someone with her all the time.
Bronwyn Herbert: She fears a one-size-fits-all model could dismantle that support.
Jo Russell: We're not against greater oversight, but you cannot say to a self-directed support model that you have to undergo exactly the same process of registration as a multinational. It's not practical.
Bronwyn Herbert: Back in Cairns, Andrew Horton says he hopes the government will listen to the concerns of smaller providers and introduce a basic registration card to verify minimum standards.
Andrew Horton: I guess we just have to make sure the reform isn't a baseball bat that attacks people it's supposed to help.
Barbara Miller: That report by Bronwyn Herbert.
Australian Broadcasting Corporation
